nativeRange.habitat: Damp, nutrient-poor soils and shallow marsh margins around streams and ponds; often submerged seasonally.

decorPreferences: open foreground with strong light penetration

goodWith: other low-tech-incompatible carpeting plants in high-tech tanks
avoidWith: substrate-disturbing fish (large cichlids, goldfish, loaches that uproot)
shrimp breeding tanks where larvae presence is critical

summary: Grass-leaved Bladderwort (Utricularia graminifolia) is a carnivorous foreground carpet plant from South and Southeast Asia, valued for its fine, grass-like lawn but demanding high light, pressurized CO2, and soft acidic water.
careNotes: Grass-leaved Bladderwort belongs to the family Lentibulariaceae — the bladderworts — a group of carnivorous plants that supplement their nutrition by trapping tiny aquatic organisms in microscopic bladder-like structures. It is generally considered an advanced foreground carpet plant, and beginners commonly struggle with it. Consistent high light, pressurized CO2, soft acidic water (pH roughly 5.5–7, low carbonate hardness), and a stable, low-nutrient substrate such as aquasoil are typically advised to encourage a dense, lawn-like mat 2–8 cm tall. New plantings often melt back in the first few weeks before re-establishing; resist trimming or re-planting during this phase. The plant is widely regarded as sensitive to parameter shifts, aggressive trimming, hard water, and algae outbreaks, all of which can trigger fresh melt episodes. The bladder traps pose no meaningful risk to adult dwarf shrimp, but they have been reported to catch shrimp larvae and very small zooplankton, so dedicated shrimp-breeding colonies may be affected. Propagation is straightforward once the plant is established — small portions of the carpet, even individual blades, can be replanted and will regrow.

additional.name: Buce Plant
additional.notes: Cross-checked lighting/CO2 requirement, soft-water preference, melting triggers, and shrimp/fish compatibility. Buce Plant collection page (https://buceplant.com/collections/aquarium-plants) did not surface UG directly; the dedicated care guide blog was used instead.

additional.name: The Aquarium Guide
additional.notes: Cross-checked native range (Burma, China, India, Sri Lanka, Thailand, Vietnam), pH 5–6.5, temperature 18–25°C, kH 4–5, family Lentibulariaceae, propagation by runners/division, and impact of carnivorous bladder traps on shrimp larvae.
